_provenance.source_pdf: Greenan, Brent - The Jazz Standards Playbook.pdf

systems.name: Functional Harmony Navigation System
systems.summary: This system governs how a jazz improviser moves through functional harmonic progressions built from the Major and Minor Diatonic Series of 7th Chords. Its members are the chord quality categories (major 7, dominant 7, minor 7, half-diminished, diminished 7) organized by their Roman numeral positions. Traversal rules specify how to move melodically between chord members using voice leading — primarily the stepwise resolution of guide tones (3rds and 7ths) from chord to chord, especially the 7th-to-3rd resolution across ii-V-I progressions. Modification rules describe how any chord member may be altered: diatonic minor chords may be converted to dominant 7ths, V chords may be replaced by tritone substitutes or backdoor dominants (bVII7), diminished 7 chords may substitute for rootless dominant b9 chords, and secondary dominant ii-V pairs may expand any diatonic chord into a momentary tonicization. The system's analytical stance is relational: every chord's function depends on the chords before and after it, never in isolation.

members.name: Major 7 Chord (Imaj7, IVmaj7)
members.summary: Tonic and subdominant function; formula Root-3-5-7; stable arrival points in ii-V-I cadences.

members.name: Dominant 7 Chord (V7, secondary dominants, backdoor bVII7)
members.summary: Primary tension chord; formula Root-3-5-b7; drives resolution; diatonic minor chords may be converted to dominant 7ths.

members.name: Minor 7 Chord (ii, iii, vi)
members.summary: Pre-dominant and tonic minor function; formula Root-b3-5-b7; serves as the ii in ii-V-I and as tonicization targets.

members.name: Half-Diminished Chord (iiø7)
members.summary: Minor ii chord in minor key ii-V-i progressions; formula Root-b3-b5-b7.

members.name: Diminished 7 Chord (passing dim7, rootless dominant b9)
members.summary: Functions as rootless dominant b9 substitute or chromatic passing chord; formula Root-b3-b5-bb7.

members.name: ii-V-I Progression Unit
members.summary: The cornerstone chord progression of jazz; the fundamental cell over which all traversal and modification rules operate.

traversal_rules.name: Guide-Tone Voice Leading (7th resolves to 3rd)
traversal_rules.summary: When moving between chords in a ii-V-I or cycle-of-fourths progression, the 7th of each chord resolves by step to the 3rd of the next chord, producing smooth melodic connection.

references.quote_excerpt: the 7th of each chord resolves to the 3rd of the next chord
Voice leading is the smooth melodic movement of notes from one chord to the next

traversal_rules.name: Arpeggio Connection via Nearest Chord Tone
traversal_rules.summary: When moving between chord arpeggios, enter the next chord on whichever chord tone is closest to the last note played, not necessarily the root.

references.topic: chord tone arpeggio voice leading rule
references.quote_excerpt: move to the nearest chord tone of the next chord
connected each arpeggio with the nearest chord tone of the proceeding chord

traversal_rules.name: Cycle-of-Fourths Harmonic Movement
traversal_rules.summary: Chords and key centers progress by descending fifths / ascending fourths as the default motion of jazz harmony, governing both analysis and improvisation planning.

references.quote_excerpt: it's a perfect study of cycling 4ths, which is an important movement in jazz repertoire
This vi chord starts a long cycle of 4ths all the way through the Dbmaj7

traversal_rules.name: Stepwise Connection at Key-Center Boundaries
traversal_rules.summary: When moving between rapidly modulating key centers (e.g., cycle of major thirds), prefer entering each new key center by half step or whole step at bar boundaries to maintain melodic flow.

references.topic: Connecting key centers by half/whole step
references.quote_excerpt: I do my best to enter into each new key center by half steps or whole steps

traversal_rules.name: Relational Chord Function Analysis
traversal_rules.summary: Always interpret a chord's function by examining both the chord before and after it; harmonic analysis is contextual, not absolute.

references.quote_excerpt: To understand the function of a chord, you must look at both the chord that comes before it and after it
the most important thing to pay attention to is where the chords are going

modification_rules.name: Diatonic Minor to Dominant 7 Conversion
modification_rules.summary: Any diatonic minor chord may be converted to a dominant 7 chord; this is a standard jazz harmonic practice that expands the pool of resolving dominants.

references.quote_excerpt: jazz musicians are constantly turning minor chords into dominant 7 chords
it is entirely normal and acceptable to turn minor chords in the diatonic series into dominant 7th chords

modification_rules.name: Tritone Substitution
modification_rules.summary: A dominant 7 chord may be replaced by the dominant 7 chord whose root lies a tritone interval away, sharing guide tones (3rd and 7th) with the original.

references.quote_excerpt: a tritone substitution, meaning, a chord is being substituted by another chord with a root a tritone interval away

modification_rules.name: Backdoor Dominant Substitution (bVII7 for V7)
modification_rules.summary: The V7 chord may be replaced by bVII7 (approaching the tonic by whole step), which shares many notes with an altered V7 and can be expanded to a full backdoor ii-V.

references.quote_excerpt: A backdoor dominant is a dominant 7th chord that substitutes the V7 chord for a bVII7 chord approaching the I chord by a whole step
the Bbmin7-Eb7 is really acting as a backdoor ii-V into the Fmaj7

modification_rules.name: Diminished 7 as Rootless Dominant b9 Substitute
modification_rules.summary: A diminished 7 chord may substitute for a dominant 7(b9) chord by functioning as its rootless voicing; all pitch classes are shared except the bass root.

references.quote_excerpt: You can think of the F#dim7 as a rootless D7(b9)
The F#dim7 chord is a substitute for the dominant VI7 chord

modification_rules.name: Secondary Dominant Tonicization (ii-V Expansion)
modification_rules.summary: Any diatonic chord may be preceded by its secondary dominant (V7/x) or a full ii-V pair, temporarily tonicizing that chord as a new tonic.

references.quote_excerpt: A secondary dominant is when a dominant 7 chord acts as a V chord of a diatonic chord other than the tonic
it's just as easy to add a ii chord in front of it to create a ii-V chord progression

modification_rules.name: Hybrid ii-V-I (Minor ii-V Resolving to Major I)
modification_rules.summary: The ii and V chords may be borrowed from the parallel minor key while resolving to the major I chord, creating an expressive surprise.

references.quote_excerpt: A hybrid ii-V-I is when a minor ii-V-i is mixed with a major ii-V-I. The ii and V are borrowed from the minor key, but they resolve to the major I
it gives you the sense that it will resolve to a minor chord, but then unexpectedly, it resolves to a major chord

modification_rules.name: Minimal-Change Voice Leading (Only Alter 3rds and 7ths)
modification_rules.summary: When navigating harmonically dense or shifting passages, adjust only the notes that define chord quality — the 3rds and 7ths — keeping all other melodic tones constant.

references.quote_excerpt: I try to adjust the notes minimally, only to accommodate the changes necessary from one chord to another
What are the defining notes of each chord? In other words, which notes differ from the chord before it

modification_rules.name: Extension Layer Permission (9ths, 11ths, 13ths)
modification_rules.summary: Any 7th chord may be extended with 9th, 11th, or 13th tones; these upper extensions are available as color tones in improvisation.

references.quote_excerpt: Utilizing the 9th, 11th and 13th can have a powerful effect

modification_rules.name: Chromatic Passing Tone Insertion
modification_rules.summary: Non-diatonic notes may be inserted between two diatonic notes by half-step to connect them; these are labeled chromatic passing tones and do not represent structural chord tones.

references.quote_excerpt: A chromatic passing tone is a non-diatonic note that connects two diatonic notes in a passage, usually by half steps
the note in question does not represent a basic chord tone, extension or alteration, but is simply chromatically approaching a diatonic note

systems.name: Modal Harmony Improvisation System
systems.summary: This system governs improvisation in modal harmonic contexts — pieces where chord movement is minimal or static and modes (rather than functional ii-V-I progressions) define the pitch language. Its members are the seven modes of the major scale, used as named pitch collections over sustained key centers. The central traversal rule is that modal arpeggios connect via the nearest chord tone rather than always starting on the root, and adjacent modes link through half- or whole-step motion. The defining modification rule is that guide tones (3rds and 7ths) are not required in modal contexts — the mode itself is the resource pool, and idiomatic modal playing means using modes as note choices rather than running them as scales. The Miles Davis approach to So What (sparse, space-conscious, phrase-then-expand, Dorian with occasional chromatic approach notes) is modeled as the aesthetic standard for this system.

members.name: Ionian (Major) — Mode 1
members.summary: Parent scale; W-W-H-W-W-W-H.

members.name: Dorian — Mode 2
members.summary: Primary mode for modal jazz; W-H-W-W-W-H-W; scale tone formula 1-2-b3-4-5-6-b7.

members.name: Phrygian — Mode 3
members.summary: H-W-W-W-H-W-W; darkest diatonic mode.

members.name: Lydian — Mode 4
members.summary: W-W-W-H-W-W-H; raised 4th gives bright, floating quality.

members.name: Mixolydian — Mode 5
members.summary: Major scale with b7; formula 1-2-3-4-5-6-b7; primary scale for dominant 7 chords.

members.name: Aeolian (Natural Minor) — Mode 6
members.summary: W-H-W-W-H-W-W; natural minor scale.

members.name: Locrian — Mode 7
members.summary: H-W-W-H-W-W-W; built on the leading tone; basis for half-diminished chord.

members.name: Whole-Half Diminished Scale
members.summary: Eight-note scale; W-H-W-H-W-H-W; used over diminished 7 and dominant b9 chords.

traversal_rules.name: Modal Arpeggio: Enter on Nearest Chord Tone
traversal_rules.summary: When connecting arpeggios across modal chord changes, begin the next arpeggio on whichever chord tone is closest to the last note played, not necessarily the root.

references.quote_excerpt: connected each arpeggio with the nearest chord tone of the proceeding chord

traversal_rules.name: Modal Pattern Exercise in 3rds with Half/Whole-Step Mode Links
traversal_rules.summary: Adjacent modes are connected through 3rd-based patterns that transition to the next mode by either a half step or whole step, internalizing mode relationships across the parent scale.

references.quote_excerpt: the pattern moves in 3rds and connects to the next mode in either a half step or a whole step

traversal_rules.name: Space-and-Expand Phrasing (Miles Davis Model)
traversal_rules.summary: In modal contexts, play a phrase, leave space, then expand upon that phrase — avoiding continuous note-running in favor of sparse, intentional melodic statements.

references.quote_excerpt: He plays a phrase, leaves some space, and then expands upon that phrase

modification_rules.name: Guide Tone Omission in Modal Contexts
modification_rules.summary: In modal harmony with minimal chord movement, guide tones (3rds and 7ths) are not required as connective tissue; the mode as pitch collection replaces the guide-tone framework.

references.topic: guide tones omitted for modal tunes
references.quote_excerpt: there is no need to identify the guide tones

modification_rules.name: Scales as Pitch Collections (Not Linear Drills)
modification_rules.summary: Modes and scales are to be used as harmonic resource pools — non-ordered sets of available note choices — rather than linear sequences to be played up and down.

references.quote_excerpt: The best way to think about the use of scales is 'pitch collections'

references.topic: modes as note choices, not scales
references.quote_excerpt: this doesn't mean you should play the scales like scales. You should just use them as note choices

modification_rules.name: Chromatic Approach Notes in Modal Playing
modification_rules.summary: Within Dorian or other modal contexts, occasional chromatic approach notes are permitted as expressive exceptions that do not disrupt modal identity.

references.quote_excerpt: He generally plays within the Dorian mode, with exceptions of course and occasional chromatic approach notes

systems.name: Ear-First Acquisition System (LIST Process)
systems.summary: This system describes the procedural method by which a jazz musician should learn any standard. Its members are the four sequential stages of the LIST process (Listen, Internalize, Sing, Transfer), each with defined entry criteria and outputs. Traversal rules specify the mandatory order of progression through the stages — instrument put away during Listen, undivided attention during Internalize, singing the melody alone as a pass/fail gate before Transfer, and melody learned before chords within the Transfer stage. The modification rules specify enhancements within stages: including vocal versions and learning lyrics to deepen melodic internalization, using Frank Sinatra as the reference source for straight melody, isolating bass notes to train harmonic ear, and using sheet music only as a verification check rather than a primary learning source.

members.name: Listen — Stage 1
members.summary: Instrument put away; deliberate listening to multiple recordings; no playing.

members.name: Internalize — Stage 2
members.summary: Undivided, distraction-free attention; treat music like a movie — full engagement.

members.name: Sing — Stage 3
members.summary: Sing melody both with recording and alone; pass/fail gate before touching instrument; optionally sing bass notes for harmony.

members.name: Transfer — Stage 4
members.summary: Melody first, then chords; find on instrument what can already be sung; chord learning via root identification then quality determination.

traversal_rules.name: LIST Stages Must Be Traversed in Order
traversal_rules.summary: The four stages must be completed in sequence — Listen before Internalize, Internalize before Sing, Sing before Transfer — with each stage's output serving as the prerequisite for the next.

references.quote_excerpt: Listen / Internalize / Sing / Transfer
Be sure that you can sing the melody of the standard, both along with the recording and on your own, before you learn it on your instrument

traversal_rules.name: Melody Before Chords Within Transfer Stage
traversal_rules.summary: Within the Transfer stage, learn the melody on the instrument first; only after melody is established move to learning chord progressions by ear.

references.quote_excerpt: Transfer the melody. Start learning the melody on your instrument

traversal_rules.name: Root Identification Before Quality Determination
traversal_rules.summary: When learning chords by ear, first identify the bass note (root), then determine the chord quality; these are two sequential cognitive operations, not simultaneous.

references.quote_excerpt: if you can identify that the bass player is playing a concert Eb note for the first chord, then all you have to do is figure out what quality it is

modification_rules.name: Include Vocal Recordings and Learn Lyrics
modification_rules.summary: During the Listen stage, always include at least one vocal version; learning lyrics deepens melodic memory, especially for instrumentalists who would otherwise skip this step.

references.quote_excerpt: Learning lyrics is extremely helpful for internalizing melodies

modification_rules.name: Use Frank Sinatra (or Original Artist) as Straight Melody Reference
modification_rules.summary: To establish a reliable, unembellished melody reference, use Frank Sinatra recordings (or the original composer/artist); avoid learning melody from artists known to heavily ornament.

references.quote_excerpt: Frank Sinatra is your friend. Frank almost always sings melodies straight

modification_rules.name: Extend Sing Stage to Bass Notes for Harmonic Training
modification_rules.summary: To add harmonic ear training, extend the Sing stage by singing the bass line (root motion) of the standard, not just the melody.

references.quote_excerpt: try singing the bass notes. This will help you begin to learn the harmony of the jazz standard

modification_rules.name: Sheet Music as Verification Tool Only
modification_rules.summary: Sheet music may be used to check what has been learned by ear, but must not be used as the primary learning source; ear comes first, notation confirms.

references.quote_excerpt: The idea is not to throw away sheet music but to know its place

systems.name: Improvisation Practice Toolkit System
systems.summary: This system organizes the concrete improvisation practice methods taught across all chapters into a structured toolkit. Its members are the named practice tools: guide tones, melody reference, chord tones/arpeggios, contrafact composition, motif development, transcribed licks, and composed solos. Traversal rules govern how a practitioner sequences or escalates across tools — beginning with harmonic reduction (guide tones alone, or single-note practice) before adding melodic complexity, scaffolding through the three-chorus melody exercise from straight melody to free improvisation, and transposing all learned material through all 12 keys for full internalization. Modification rules govern how each tool may be altered or combined.

members.name: Guide Tones Practice Tool
members.summary: Isolated half- or quarter-note 3rds and 7ths through the full song form; primary harmonic-melodic spine.

members.name: Melody Reference Tool
members.summary: Using the tune's written melody as a guideline during improvisation; scaffolds departure from fully straight to freely improvised.

members.name: Chord Tones / Arpeggios Tool
members.summary: Playing root, 3rd, 5th, and 7th as ordered arpeggios through changes; voice-led to nearest tone at each transition.

members.name: Contrafact Composition Tool
members.summary: A new melody composed over existing chord changes; may be simple/singable or bebop-style; must contain thematic repetition and structure.

members.name: Motif Development Tool
members.summary: Short rhythmic or melodic cells repeated and varied throughout a solo to create coherence over complex changes.

members.name: Transcribed Licks Tool
members.summary: Licks learned from recordings of great jazz musicians, internalized and transposed through 12 keys.

members.name: Composed Solos Tool
members.summary: Full solos composed at slow pace (written or memorized on instrument) to develop jazz language and phrase construction.

traversal_rules.name: Three-Chorus Melody Escalation
traversal_rules.summary: Practice escalates across three choruses: first chorus plays melody straight, second chorus embellishes it as a guideline, third chorus improvises freely while referencing melodic fragments.

references.quote_excerpt: The first chorus, just play the melody completely straight

traversal_rules.name: All Licks and Patterns Through All 12 Keys
traversal_rules.summary: Every lick, pattern, or harmonic concept must be practiced in all 12 keys to achieve full internalization and strengthen weak key areas.

references.quote_excerpt: I always suggest taking things through all 12 keys. It's a powerful practice that not only helps you internalize musical information

traversal_rules.name: Density Reduction for Harmonically Dense Passages
traversal_rules.summary: When facing confusing chord changes, reduce note density radically — down to a single note over an entire sequence — to isolate chord-tone hearing before adding complexity.

references.quote_excerpt: try playing as few notes as possible over a given chord progression. Sometimes it can even have a profound effect to play just one note over an entire sequence

references.topic: keep it simple over confusing changes
references.quote_excerpt: Whenever I come across a section within a piece of music that seems confusing

traversal_rules.name: Quarter-Note Solo Exercise (Rhythmic Reduction)
traversal_rules.summary: Practice improvisation using only quarter notes to remove rhythmic complexity, isolating harmonic navigation as the sole focus.

references.quote_excerpt: I stick to a quarter note rhythm in all of my note choices. I take out the rhythmic component from my improvisation and just focus on navigating the changes

modification_rules.name: Contrafact Singability and Simplicity Requirement
modification_rules.summary: A student contrafact should be simple enough to sing, should outline chord changes through chord tones, and must express a coherent theme; complexity is secondary to melodic logic.

references.quote_excerpt: I tried to keep the melody as simple as I could. My goal was to create a sing-able melody

modification_rules.name: Bebop Contrafact Mandatory Structure Rule
modification_rules.summary: Even a busy bebop-style contrafact must contain repeated sections, rhythmic clones, and deliberate structure; stream-of-consciousness is not acceptable.

references.quote_excerpt: note the themes throughout. There are repeated sections, rhythmic clones and general structure

modification_rules.name: Thematic Repetition with Variation (Space and Breathing)
modification_rules.summary: Strong improvisation includes recurring rhythmic and melodic themes with variations, and leaves deliberate space for the melody to breathe.

references.quote_excerpt: There are re-occurring rhythmic and melodic themes throughout with variations

modification_rules.name: Strong Melody Licenses Harmonic Freedom (Including Outside Playing)
modification_rules.summary: Any degree of harmonic freedom — including playing outside the chord changes — is permitted provided the improvised line is melodically strong and resolves with intention.

references.quote_excerpt: You could even play outside of the chord changes, but if your improvised melody is strong and you resolve it confidently, it's going to sound intentional

modification_rules.name: Note-Mapping Scaffold for Dense Harmonic Passages
modification_rules.summary: Over harmonically complex material, map out all available scale and chord tones as a conceptual scaffold before building melodic lines.

references.quote_excerpt: it can be beneficial to take a step back and map out the notes we have available to us
